meta.title: Proper use of Goroutines with pprof: Memory Leaks and Data Races
meta.description: Guide on proper use of Goroutines in Go, detecting memory leaks with pprof and eliminating data races with the race detector.
meta.ogTitle: Proper use of Goroutines with pprof: Memory Leaks and Data Races
meta.ogDescription: Guide on proper use of Goroutines in Go, detecting memory leaks with pprof and eliminating data races.

content:
Goroutines are one of Go's greatest superpowers, but also one of the most common sources of bugs in production. Memory leaks from orphaned goroutines and data races from concurrent access without synchronization are problems that every Go team eventually encounters. In this article we'll see how to identify, debug, and prevent them using pprof and Go's race detector.

The problem: goroutines that never die

Go does not have a native mechanism to kill goroutines externally. A goroutine that blocks waiting on a channel, a select with no ready case, or an infinite time.Sleep, stays in memory forever. Over time, these goroutines accumulate memory and resources, causing OOM kills and performance degradation.

// Classic leak example: goroutine trapped on a channel
func leakExample() {
  ch := make(chan int)
  go func() {
    val := <-ch  // Nothing will ever be received
    fmt.Println(val)
  }()
  // The goroutine stays blocked forever
  // ch never receives a value
}

Detecting leaks with pprof

pprof is the profiling tool included in Go. To detect goroutine leaks, you need to expose the /debug/pprof/ endpoint in your application and take periodic snapshots.

package main

import (
  "net/http"
  _ "net/http/pprof"  // Side-effect import to register handlers
  "runtime"
  "time"
)

func main() {
  // Start pprof server on a separate port
  go func() {
    http.ListenAndServe(":6060", nil)
  }()

  // Simulate a leaking goroutine
  for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
    go leakyGoroutine()
  }

  select {}
}

func leakyGoroutine() {
  // This goroutine retains memory forever
  m := make(map[int][]byte)
  for i := 0; i < 1000; i++ {
    m[i] = make([]byte, 1024) // 1KB each
  }
  // Never freed: m stays on heap
  time.Sleep(time.Hour)
}

Taking goroutine profiles

With the pprof server running, you can take multiple snapshots and compare them:

# Take goroutine profile (first snapshot)
curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=2 > goroutines_before.txt

# Wait 5 minutes
sleep 300

# Second snapshot
curl -s http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine?debug=2 > goroutines_after.txt

# Count goroutines in each snapshot
grep -c "goroutine" goroutines_before.txt
grep -c "goroutine" goroutines_after.txt

If the number of goroutines grows over time, you have a leak. But the most effective way is to use the interactive tool:

# Interactive goroutine profile
go tool pprof http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/goroutine

# In the interactive console:
# (pprof) top  — shows the top goroutines by count
# (pprof) tree — call tree
# (pprof) list <func> — source code of a specific function

Tip: For production, never expose pprof publicly. Use an internal port (localhost:6060) or authentication via a sidecar. Better yet, take profiles on demand through a protected endpoint.

Heap profile: traditional memory leaks

Goroutines are not the only cause of leaks. Objects that are accidentally kept referenced also cause them. The pprof heap profile shows you where memory is being allocated:

# Heap profile
go tool pprof -http=:8081 http://localhost:6060/debug/pprof/heap

This opens a web interface with flame graphs, top-by-consumption, and call graphs. Classic heap leak patterns:

  • Slices that grow unbounded: accumulation of unconsumed events
  • Caches without TTL: maps that store results with no size limit
  • Closures that capture large variables: the reference prevents the GC from freeing memory
  • Substrings: in Go, s[:n] retains the entire original string, not just the slice
// Leak from a growing slice
type EventProcessor struct {
  events []Event  // Never cleaned up
}

func (ep *EventProcessor) Process(e Event) {
  ep.events = append(ep.events, e)
  // Memory grows indefinitely
}

// Solution: bounded buffer or channel
type SafeProcessor struct {
  events chan Event  // Fixed capacity
}

func (sp *SafeProcessor) Start(ctx context.Context) {
  go func() {
    for {
      select {
      case e := <-sp.events:
        // process
      case <-ctx.Done():
        return
      }
    }
  }()
}

Safe patterns for goroutines

1. Always use context.Context

Context allows you to safely cancel goroutines. It is the standard mechanism for avoiding orphaned goroutines.

func worker(ctx context.Context) {
  for {
    select {
    case <-ctx.Done():
      log.Println("worker: cancelled")
      return
    default:
      // Do work
      doWork()
    }
  }
}

func main() {
  ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
  defer cancel()

  go worker(ctx)
  go worker(ctx)

  time.Sleep(10 * time.Second)
  // Both goroutines are automatically cancelled after 5s
}

2. Prefer errgroup over sync.WaitGroup

errgroup from the standard library (golang.org/x/sync/errgroup) handles cancellation and error propagation automatically.

import "golang.org/x/sync/errgroup"

func main() {
  g, ctx := errgroup.WithContext(context.Background())

  for _, task := range tasks {
    task := task  // Local capture
    g.Go(func() error {
      return task.Run(ctx)
    })
  }

  // Wait for all goroutines or the first error
  if err := g.Wait(); err != nil {
    log.Fatalf("group error: %v", err)
  }
}

3. Buffered channels and select with default

An unbuffered channel can block a goroutine if there is no receiver. Always use select with default for non-blocking operations, or buffered channels.

// Pattern: buffered channel + select to avoid blocking
type Pool struct {
  jobs chan Job
}

func NewPool(size int) *Pool {
  return &Pool{
    jobs: make(chan Job, size), // Buffer prevents blocking
  }
}

func (p *Pool) TrySubmit(j Job) bool {
  select {
  case p.jobs <- j:
    return true
  default:
    // Pool full, does not block
    return false
  }
}

Data Races: the silent enemy

A data race occurs when two goroutines access the same variable without synchronization and at least one of them writes. The result is unpredictable: from crashes to corrupt data with no visible symptoms.

// Classic data race
var counter int

func main() {
  for i := 0; i < 1000; i++ {
    go func() {
      counter++ // Race condition: simultaneous read and write
    }()
  }
  time.Sleep(time.Second)
  fmt.Println(counter) // Unpredictable value
}

Detection with -race

Go includes a built-in race detector. It is activated with the -race flag in tests or builds:

# Run tests with race detector
go test -race ./...

# Run the application with race detector
go run -race main.go

# Build with race detector
go build -race -o app main.go

When the detector finds a race, it prints:

  • The goroutine that writes and the line of code
  • The goroutine that reads and the line of code
  • The full stack trace of both
WARNING: DATA RACE
Read at 0x00c0000b4020 by goroutine 8:
  main.main.func1()
      /app/main.go:12 +0x3d

Previous write at 0x00c0000b4020 by goroutine 7:
  main.main.func1()
      /app/main.go:12 +0x51

Goroutine 8 (running) created at:
  main.main()
      /app/main.go:11 +0x4a

Goroutine 7 (finished) created at:
  main.main()
      /app/main.go:11 +0x4a

Important: -race has CPU overhead (~5-10x) and memory overhead. Do not use race detector builds in production. Use it exclusively in tests and staging.

Strategies to eliminate data races

1. Mutex (sync.Mutex / sync.RWMutex)

type SafeCounter struct {
  mu sync.Mutex
  n  int
}

func (sc *SafeCounter) Increment() {
  sc.mu.Lock()
  defer sc.mu.Unlock()
  sc.n++
}

func (sc *SafeCounter) Value() int {
  sc.mu.Lock()
  defer sc.mu.Unlock()
  return sc.n
}

2. Channels for data ownership

Instead of sharing memory, communicate it through channels. The goroutine that receives the data is the sole owner.

type CounterService struct {
  req chan struct{}
  res chan int
}

func NewCounterService() *CounterService {
  cs := &CounterService{
    req: make(chan struct{}),
    res: make(chan int),
  }
  go cs.run() // only goroutine that touches the counter
  return cs
}

func (cs *CounterService) run() {
  var counter int
  for range cs.req {
    counter++
    cs.res <- counter
  }
}

func (cs *CounterService) Increment() int {
  cs.req <- struct{}{}
  return <-cs.res
}

3. sync.Map for concurrent maps

For maps with high read/write concurrency, sync.Map is optimized:

var cache sync.Map

func GetOrStore(key string, valueFn func() interface{}) interface{} {
  v, _ := cache.LoadOrStore(key, valueFn())
  return v
}

CI/CD Integration

The race detector should run on every Pull Request. Configure it in GitHub Actions:

name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-go@v5
        with:
          go-version: "1.23"
      - run: go test -race -count=1 -shuffle=on ./...

The -count=1 flag disables test caching and -shuffle=on varies execution order, increasing the chance of detecting intermittent races.

Conclusion

Goroutines are powerful but dangerous without the right tools. pprof lets you detect memory leaks from orphaned goroutines and heap leaks, while the race detector eliminates data races before they reach production. The combination of both tools, along with patterns like context.Context, errgroup, and buffered channels, forms the foundation of a solid concurrent system in Go.

Golden rules:

  1. Every goroutine must have a cancellation mechanism (context, channel close, or timeout)
  2. Every shared variable must be protected by a mutex, channel, or sync.Map
  3. The race detector must run in CI, not just locally
  4. pprof must be available in staging to diagnose leaks early
